heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO XVII
card_summary: This chunk of Campanini's introductory essay covers the final decades of Francesco Zorzi's biography, from his involvement in the "King's Great Matter" — Henry VIII's divorce from Catherine of Aragon — to his role as architectural consultant for the rebuilding of San Francesco della Vigna in Venice (1533–34), and closes with a detailed survey of his last major work, the In Scripturam sacram Problemata (1536). Zorzi's connections to the Venetian Jewish community (Eliah Menachem Chalfan, Baruch Axelrad, Calo Calonimos) and to Christian Kabbalists around Cornelio Agrippa are documented, including a 1532 letter describing Zorzi's private Kabbalistic library. The Problemata's six-volume structure is outlined, showing how Zorzi harmonised Scripture, Kabbalah (especially the Zohar), Platonism, Hermeticism, Orphism, Pythagoreanism, and Zoroastrianism through the figure of the "seven sages" — Solomon, Job, Hermes Trismegistus, Plato, Orpheus, Pythagoras, and Zoroaster.
essay:
## Zorzi and the King's Great Matter

The chunk opens in media res, recounting Zorzi's entanglement in the dispute over Henry VIII's divorce from Catherine of Aragon. The formal legal crux was the apparent contradiction between Leviticus and Deuteronomy concerning the levirate — the obligation (or prohibition) of a man marrying his deceased brother's widow. Thomas Cranmer and Henry VIII therefore sought opinions from any authoritative source that might support the nullity of the marriage, including Jewish legal scholars.

Richard Croke was despatched to Italy and Venice to recruit consultants and to fund the purchase of books and documents. Campanini emphasises that this episode confirms Zorzi's dense network of contacts within the Venetian Jewish world:

> Ancora una volta troviamo Zorzi strettamente legato ad esponenti del mondo ebraico, alcuni dei quali ebrei convertiti, come l'ex rabbino Marco Raphael, ma anche alcuni dei più importanti ebrei veneziani, come Eliah Menachem Chalfan, Baruch (Benedetto) Axelrad e Calo Calonimos.

<cite>p. 17</cite>

Calo Calonimos is singled out as particularly significant: he was the father-in-law of Chalfan and had completed Avraham de Balmes's Hebrew-Latin grammatical work *Miqneh Avram* (*Peculium Abrae*), published by Daniel Bomberg in 1523 — one of the landmark productions of Renaissance Hebrew scholarship.

Campanini notes a hypothesis that Zorzi, despite Croke's reports insisting on his personal disinterest, may have benefited from the bibliographic acquisitions made possible by English royal funds, "in particolare nel campo dei rarissimi e preziosi manoscritti cabbalistici" (p. 17). By 1531, summoned to Rome by the pope and under pressure from the Venetian government, Zorzi quietly withdrew from the controversy without recanting his support for Henry — a prudent silence. His candidacy for the bishopric of Brescia (1531) predictably failed for want of political support.

---

## The 1532 Letter and Zorzi's Kabbalistic Library

A 1532 letter, published anonymously among the works of Cornelio Agrippa but attributed by Campanini to Bernardo Paltrinieri (majordomo of Cardinal Lorenzo Campeggi), documents a chance meeting between the letter-writer and Zorzi near Padua. The letter is quoted at length and is among the most vivid documentary testimonies to Zorzi's scholarly persona:

> Ho potuto godere della sua gentilezza nel corso di una brevissima conversazione, un poco di fretta e per non più di un quarto d'ora, anche se perfino un tempo lunghissimo non sarebbe bastato per i misteri di cui abbiamo parlato.

<cite>p. 18</cite>

The writer notes that Zorzi possessed Hebrew books both he and Agrippa had long sought, and that Zorzi offered to show them — for reading, not copying, as copying would take too long and be impractical. The letter also records that Zorzi promised "la cabala di Samuele" but warned it would be of little use. Campanini draws two conclusions: first, the letter documents that Christian Kabbalah was sustained by a network of enthusiasts bound by common passion; second, it reveals both the richness of Zorzi's private Kabbalistic collection and his characteristic caution in sharing it.

---

## San Francesco della Vigna and Sacred Architecture

In 1533–34 Zorzi was appointed *procuratore della fabbrica* — a supervisory role — for the reconstruction of the church of San Francesco della Vigna in Venice. The project was initiated by Doge Andrea Gritti as a statement of Venetian symbolic prestige, with Jacopo Sansovino as architect. Zorzi intervened against Sansovino's proportions at the outset; the dispute led the doge to request a formal memorandum from Zorzi laying out his counter-proposals.

The resulting document is described by Campanini as a characteristic synthesis of Zorzi's intellectual world:

> Il documento, composto secondo una caratteristica combinazione di numerologia platonica (con citazioni quasi letterali della celebre pagina del Timeo di Platone intorno al valore metafisico di talune proporzioni numeriche) e speculazioni cabbalistiche sulla corrispondenza mistica tra il tempio, qui rappresentato dalla chiesa, e il corpo umano, fu sottoscritto non solo da Sansovino, ma anche da tutti i grandi artisti coinvolti nel progetto.

<cite>p. 19</cite>

Signatories included Fortunio Spira, Sebastiano Serlio, and Tiziano Vecellio (Titian). The church was completed within a few years; the facade was added later, around 1572, by Andrea Palladio. This episode crystallises the *De Harmonia Mundi* system in applied form: Platonic number theory from the *Timaeus* and Kabbalistic typology of the Temple-as-body are used to determine the literal proportions of a building.

Campanini also notes that Zorzi, in his *Commento sopra l'Elegante Poema*, records having designed three roads leading to the Amadite convent of San Gerolamo on the hill of Asolo, with fountains, conceived as a meditative complement to the ascent — a further instance of sacred spatial design.

---

## The *In Scripturam Sacram Problemata* (1536)

The final section of the chunk outlines Zorzi's last major published work, the *In Scripturam sacram Problemata* (Venice: Bernardino Vitali, 1536), dedicated to Pope Paul III. The work contains three thousand questions (*problemata*), ordered broadly on the biblical text, with systematic recourse to Kabbalistic literature, especially the Zohar.

The six-volume structure is:
1. **De historiis sacramentalibus** — the narrative history of the Old Testament
2. **De lege** — the non-narrative legislative parts of the Old Testament
3. **De oraculis prophetarum** — the prophets
4. **De Evangelio** — the Gospels
5. **De septem sapientium doctrina** — doctrine of the "seven sages"
6. **De multigenis rebus occurrentibus** — miscellaneous themes, including angels, virtues of plants, animals, and places

The fifth volume is particularly significant for Zorzi's cosmological synthesis. The "seven sages" are not the canonical ancient Greek seven sages of doxographical tradition, but a Zorzi-specific biblical-Platonic-Hermetic combination:

> una combinazione biblico-platonico-ermetica tipica del pensiero zorziano, associando Salomone e Giobbe a Ermete Trismegisto, Platone, Orfeo, Pitagora e Zoroastro.

<cite>p. 20</cite>

This grouping encapsulates Zorzi's *prisca theologia* programme: ancient wisdom traditions — Hermetic, Orphic, Pythagorean, Zoroastrian — are treated as consonant with, indeed converging upon, the biblical revelation. The sixth volume addresses mysteries in both divine realities (angels) and natural ones (the hidden virtues of plants, animals, and places), bringing the work into contact with natural magic and the broader occult philosophy of the period.

The chunk closes noting that the *Problemata* continues the alchemical interest already present in the *De Harmonia Mundi*, though Zorzi observes that alchemists' promises are rarely fulfilled — a fragment that the chunk leaves incomplete. Throughout, Campanini presents Zorzi's biography as consistently shaped by the intersection of Franciscan observance, Christian Kabbalah, Venetian political culture, and the Neoplatonic-Hermetic synthesis that defines the *De Harmonia Mundi* project.
